source_title: The Most Effective Weight Training, Cardio & Nutrition for Women | Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: As a woman, if I honestly thought there were things we should do differently to optimize our results, of course I would be doing them myself and telling other women to do them too. The narrative that women need a sex-specific program makes women feel like they're being spoken to and considered. But the data says men and women respond to exercise very similarly.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple is an expert in both the science and practice of building muscle and strength, cardiovascular fitness, and the relationship between hormones and exercise. Today's discussion is focused on fitness for women and how it overlaps directly with the same things that men should do for their fitness.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Is there anything fundamentally different about muscle tissue in men and women? In a way that should change people's behavior about how to exercise?

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: When we look at muscle protein synthesis in response to exercise or nutrition, there are no differences. Very similar protein metabolism response, very similar growth response. The major difference is the baseline muscularity. During puberty, when men experience a surge in testosterone, that coincides with an increase in muscle mass. However, once they start training, they will gain similar relative size.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: If a male is somewhere in the reference range—300 to 900 nanograms per deciliter—a male with 400 versus 600, it's not as if the male with 600 can make that much more muscle growth. And the same is true for women?

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: That's right. As long as you're within that broad normal range. There isn't this clear linear relationship where we would say, oh, let's measure your testosterone. You are going to be a hard gainer or you're going to put on mass really easily.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: For a woman who's not currently doing resistance training, at what age is too early to do resistance training? What does the data say?

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: It's not that it's unsafe for teenage girls to lift weights. It's just that it wasn't done, and so people weren't teaching them. There's a lot of advantages, especially if you're playing sports, because we have female athletes who can see not only a performance improvement but also an injury reduction from doing some resistance training. It's certainly valuable to start younger. That said, you can gain muscle anytime, even if you start at 70.

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: If you're only going to train two or three times a week, I think it's better to do a full-body session because you're hitting all those major muscle groups at an appropriate number of times per week. We need to challenge the muscle in a way that is a sufficient stimulus for growth. If we finish that set of 10 and we could easily do another 10, then that load is too light.

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: At least two sets after a sufficient warm-up. I prefer three. You could do four. Beyond that is probably overkill. For rest intervals, two minutes is probably fine for most people. Maybe three minutes for something like a squat or deadlift.

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: We used to think there was a strength endurance continuum. Our understanding has evolved. If the goal is hypertrophy we have way more flexibility because we do see similar growth whether you're training in those high load low rep ranges or the lower load high rep ranges, provided that we train close enough to failure.

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: The short answer is no—women should not train differently depending on where they are in their menstrual cycle. The conversation is good, but the shift of saying because hormones are fluctuating you need to change how you are exercising is way too simplistic and doesn't align with the data. Train hard, train consistently, train progressively. You are not less capable on any day because you have your menstrual period.

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: When it comes to combined oral contraceptive pills and strength, hypertrophy, power, we're not seeing them move the needle in either direction. Similar to the influence of endogenous hormone fluctuations, which are quite substantial, if those aren't affecting performance or exercise-induced adaptations, it's not that shocking that hormonal contraceptives wouldn't move the needle to a great extent either.

paragraphs.speaker: lauren
paragraphs.name: Dr. Lauren Colenso-Semple
paragraphs.text: There's no reason to change your training during perimenopause or menopause. We still want the same adaptations—increase and maintain muscle size and strength, reduce fall risk and fracture risk, maintain bone density. We see age-related muscle loss that is exacerbated by physical inactivity. But when you look at lean mass across the menopause transition, that in and of itself isn't accelerating the loss of muscle.
